one_line_takeaway: TINA shows that state-of-the-art concept-erasure/unlearning for diffusion models only cuts the text-to-image link, not the visual knowledge: a text-free, optimization-based null-text DDIM inversion reliably regenerates 'erased' nudity, styles, and objects even from defenses built to resist text attacks.
key_finding: TINA achieves the highest attack success rate (ASR) against essentially every defense tested, and the gap is largest precisely against the 'robust' defenses designed to resist text-based attacks. On nudity (Table 1) TINA reaches 82.39% vs ESD, 97.89% vs FMN, 92.96% vs MACE, and critically 78.87% vs AdvUnlearn, 71.13% vs SalUn, 80.99% vs STEREO, where text-based baselines collapse (UDA 23.24% on AdvUnlearn, RAB 0.00% on SalUn). On object erasure (Table 4) text-based attacks fail almost completely on modern defenses (P4D/UDA/CCE in the single digits on Scissorhands and STEREO) while TINA stays uniformly high (78.0% Scissorhands, 72.0% STEREO, 68.0% EraseDiff). A t-SNE analysis shows the optimized noises z*_T look like random noise (no concept separation) yet elicit clearly concept-separable internal UNet mid-block activations, evidencing that the forbidden visual knowledge persists and is deterministically re-activated without any text.
core_contribution: The paper introduces TINA (Text-free INversion Attack), an attack that regenerates concepts that were supposedly 'erased' from text-to-image diffusion models via concept-erasure/unlearning methods. Its central claim is that existing concept-erasure defenses are text-centric: they sever the text-to-image mapping but leave the underlying visual knowledge intact in the parameters. TINA substantiates this by bypassing the text-conditioning pathway entirely. Given a concept-erased model and a target image of the forbidden concept, TINA uses an optimization-based DDIM inversion under a null-text (empty) condition to find a specific initial noise vector z*_T that, when fed back through standard deterministic DDIM sampling under the same null-text condition, deterministically regenerates the forbidden concept. The authors position this as the first attack to identify and exploit a text-free generative pathway, contrasting with all prior attacks (both discrete-token and embedding-space) that remain tied to the text-conditioning mechanism.
threat_model: White-box adversary with full access to the concept-erased/sanitized diffusion model (needs gradients through the UNet for the inner optimization). The adversary also needs a target/reference image exemplifying the concept to invert; in the paper these ground-truth images are generated from the original pre-erasure Stable Diffusion v1.4 checkpoint. The attacker does NOT need any working text prompt for the concept, since the whole point is to operate under a null-text condition and evade text-centric defenses. Goal: force the deployed erased model to (re)produce forbidden content (nudity, a copyrighted artistic style such as Van Gogh, or an erased object class such as tench) despite the unlearning safeguard.
limitations: The attack assumes white-box access to the erased model (gradients required) and a reference target image of the forbidden concept; in the paper these targets are produced by the ORIGINAL pre-erasure SD v1.4, so obtaining them presupposes access to the un-erased model or equivalent samples — a strong assumption the paper does not foreground. It is computationally heavy: an inner optimization loop of K=25 gradient steps at each of T=50 inversion steps per image. Evaluation is limited to Stable Diffusion v1.4 and to a narrow concept slice per task (I2P nudity, Van Gogh style only, tench object class only), so generalization across model families and broader concepts is untested. Some defenses appear only in some tables (per-task applicability), and STEREO style ASR (44.0%) shows the method is not uniformly dominant. The latent-embedding analysis is qualitative (t-SNE) rather than a quantitative separability measure. No defense/mitigation is proposed beyond the high-level call to operate on internal visual representations.

datasets_used: Stable Diffusion v1.4 (base checkpoint used to generate ground-truth target images and as the model under all defenses)
ESD art-related evaluation prompts for Van Gogh style
Imagenette 'tench' object class with GPT-4-generated prompts (following UnlearnDiffAtk)
WikiArt (used to fine-tune the 129-class ViT-base style classifier)
ImageNet (pretraining for the ViT style classifier and the ResNet-50 object classifier)
quantitative_results: Nudity ASR (Table 1): TINA 82.39% ESD, 97.89% FMN, 82.39% UCE, 92.96% MACE, 80.28% RECE, 78.87% AdvUnlearn, 71.13% SalUn, 80.99% STEREO
Nudity robust-defense contrast: UDA 23.24% and RAB 2.11% on AdvUnlearn; RAB 0.00% and CCE 2.82% on SalUn (vs TINA 78.87% / 71.13%)
Style ASR (Table 2): TINA 70.0% ESD, 72.0% FMN, 74.0% AC, 72.0% MACE, 80.0% SPM, 74.0% RECE, 70.0% AdvUnlearn, 44.0% STEREO; UDA only 32.0% and CCE 8.0% on ESD; P4D/UDA 0.0% on STEREO

Object ASR (Table 4): TINA 70.0% ESD, 68.0% EraseDiff, 72.0% SalUn, 78.0% Scissorhands, 72.0% STEREO; text baselines in single digits on Scissorhands/STEREO (e.g., P4D 6.0% Scissorhands, UDA 2.0% STEREO)
Config: T=50 DDIM steps, inner loop K=25 iterations, AdamW lr=0.001, CFG scale 7.5, single NVIDIA A100
